planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in dry to moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; this is one of the few viburnums that thrives and fruits in full dry shade under mature trees.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; mapleleaf viburnum is exceptional among viburnums for its tolerance of both dry conditions and full shade — characteristics that together make it one of the most useful native shrubs for planting under established deciduous trees where root competition and low light challenge most plants. The maple-like leaves turn brilliant pink to rose-pink to crimson-magenta in fall, some of the most vivid fall color of any shade-tolerant shrub. Flat-topped white flower clusters in late spring attract native bees, followed by small dark blue-black berries (technically edible but not palatable) that are consumed by songbirds. Spreads slowly by rhizome to form naturalistic colonies; one of the best native shrubs for difficult dry shade situations.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in well-drained, alkaline to slightly acidic, rocky to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; rusty blackhaw is particularly well-adapted to limestone soils of the Southeast and Gulf Coast.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, rocky or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; rusty blackhaw is one of the most beautiful and underused native fruiting shrubs of the Southeast, producing spectacular flat clusters of creamy white flowers in early spring, followed by clusters of dark blue-black berries with a raisin-like, sweet-tart flavor that can be eaten fresh or used for jelly and wine. The leaves have rusty-red fuzzy undersides (giving the species name) and turn deep burgundy-red in fall. It grows naturally on dry, rocky, limestone slopes and woodland edges and is extremely drought tolerant once established. The berries provide fall food for mockingbirds, bluebirds, and thrushes. It can be trained as a specimen shrub or allowed to develop into a small multi-trunk tree.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil at the water's edge or in shallow water up to 4 inches deep; fall planting allows roots to establish before spring bloom.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil at the margin of a pond, stream, or rain garden in partial shade to full sun; southern blue flag is the native wetland iris of the coastal South and Southeast, growing naturally in swamps, freshwater marshes, wet meadows, and pond margins from Virginia to Florida. The violet-blue flowers veined with white and yellow in late spring are extraordinarily beautiful and important for early bumblebees and native bee species large enough to pollinate the structured flowers. The bold, sword-like leaves provide vertical structure in water gardens. Plant where the rhizome can stay moist — unlike bearded irises, this species must not dry out. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested. A superb companion to pickerel rush, arrowhead, and swamp lily in water garden designs.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter or container trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil; plant two or more trees for better fruit set. Seeds require cold stratification for 90 days.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; black cherry is arguably the most ecologically important fruiting tree in eastern North America, supporting the larvae of over 400 species of moths and butterflies (more than almost any other tree genus), providing early-spring nectar in its white flower racemes, and producing thousands of small black cherries in summer that are consumed by over 70 species of birds — robins, waxwings, thrushes, catbirds, woodpeckers — as well as bears, foxes, and raccoons. The small black cherries are edible and intensely flavorful, traditionally used for cherry wine, jelly, and syrups. The wilted leaves and seeds contain prussic acid and are toxic to livestock. The bark has traditional medicinal use as a cough suppressant. A fast-growing native tree (2–3 feet per year) that can seed prolifically.

planting.note: Plant container-grown palms in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; extremely slow growing but very long-lived. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; needle palm is the world's most cold-hardy palm, surviving temperatures as low as 0°F to -5°F — making it the only truly cold-hardy palm for zone 7 and one of the outstanding choices for providing a tropical look in surprisingly cold climates. It grows naturally in the understory of moist coastal plain forests and swamp margins in the southeast US (Florida, Georgia, South Carolina) and is adapted to significant shade. It is extremely slow growing (3–6 inches per year) and develops into a clumping, stemless or short-stemmed palm with dramatic fan-shaped leaves. The sharp black needle-like spines on the leaf sheaths deter both deer and humans. Excellent for creating tropical texture in a shaded southern garden.
